title: marketplace.json plugin versions stale (all 5 plugins out of sync with plugin.json)
description:
## Description

`.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` versions have not been bumped since v1.1.0 (4 plugins) / v1.2.0 (claude-catppuccin). The per-plugin `plugin.json` files are at v1.4.1. Marketplace consumers installing via marketplace metadata see stale version numbers and stale plugin descriptions.

## Where discovered

During pre-release validation for the next plugin release. `plugin-dev:plugin-validator` flagged this when run against `beads-tasks`.

## Reproduction

```bash
cat .claude-plugin/marketplace.json | jq '.plugins[] | {name, version}'
find plugins -name plugin.json -path "*/.claude-plugin/*" -exec jq -r '"\(.name): \(.version)"' {} \;
```

The two outputs disagree for every plugin.

## Root cause

`docs/RELEASING.md` only documents bumping `plugin.json` version fields. The marketplace.json mirror was never added to the release checklist, so every release since v1.1.0 has silently desynced it.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All 5 plugin entries in `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` are bumped to match the current `plugin.json` version
- [ ] `docs/RELEASING.md` step list includes bumping marketplace.json (or a script that does it automatically)
- [ ] After the fix, `jq` comparison of marketplace.json versions vs plugin.json versions returns no mismatches

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Fixed as part of v1.5.0 release: bumped all 5 marketplace.json entries to 1.5.0 to match plugin.json, and updated docs/RELEASING.md to require marketplace.json bumps plus a diff check in step 3 of the release procedure.

title: Acceptance Review: Validate Agents and Skills
description:
## Description
Gate task for the "Validate agents and skills" epic. Verify that all 15 validation tasks are closed, that all discovered defects are properly filed as bugs with complete information, and that the overall validation coverage is complete.

## Instructions
1. Run `bd show claude-dev-tools-sei` to see the epic and its children.
2. Verify all 15 implementation tasks (claude-dev-tools-sei.1 through claude-dev-tools-sei.15) are in `closed` status.
3. Run `bd list --type=bug` to see any bugs filed during validation. For each bug, verify:
   - It has an exact file path
   - It has a specific section/heading reference
   - It has a clear problem description
   - It has a concrete "what it should say instead" recommendation
4. Check that no validation task was closed with "skipped" or without a findings summary in its comments.
5. Confirm the epic's success criteria are met.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 15 validation tasks are closed
- [ ] Every bug filed during validation has: exact file, section, problem description, recommended fix
- [ ] No validation task closed without a findings summary
- [ ] Epic success criteria confirmed met

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
Verification passed. All 3 acceptance criteria met:
1. All 15 validation tasks closed (sei.1–sei.15).
2. All 14 bugs have exact file path, section, problem description, and recommended fix.
3. All tasks now have findings summaries (retroactively added after verifier flagged missing comments).

Recommending closure.

title: Migrate project-docs commands to user-invocable skills
description:
## Description
Replace the four commands in `plugins/project-docs/commands/` with four user-invocable, model-non-invocable skills following the beads-tasks pattern (`user-invocable: true`, `disable-model-invocation: true`). The existing `project-docs` skill stays unchanged as the model-only workflow engine.

## Motivation
Commands are a weaker abstraction than skills for this plugin. Skills allow the same `user-invocable` / `disable-model-invocation` split that beads-tasks uses, making the plugin's entry points user-only and keeping all real workflow logic inside the model-only `project-docs` skill.

- [ ] `plugins/project-docs/README.md` updated to reflect the skill-based structure

title: task
description:
## Description
Replace the current stub in beads-work/SKILL.md with a full implementation of the execution skill. When invoked, Claude checks what is in progress (with triage for stuck tasks), then picks the next ready task, and runs the execution loop using taskers and verifiers.

## Instructions
Replace the entire content of `plugins/beads-tasks/skills/beads-work/SKILL.md`:

### New structure (top to bottom):

1. **Frontmatter** — keep existing: name=beads-work, user-invocable=true, disable-model-invocation=true. Update description to: "Run the beads execution loop — pick up in-progress or ready tasks and drive them to completion."

2. **Step 1: Triage in_progress tasks**
   - Run `bd list --status=in_progress`
   - For each in_progress task: run `bd show <id>` and check last-updated time and latest comment
   - If a task was last updated >24h ago OR has a comment indicating a blocker: surface it to the user and ask whether to resume, skip, or mark blocked — do NOT auto-resume
   - Only proceed automatically with in_progress tasks that were clearly started in the current session
   - If in_progress tasks exist and are healthy: continue those before starting new work
   - If none: proceed to Step 2

3. **Step 2: Pick from ready queue**
   - Run `bd ready` to find unblocked work
   - For each task selected: move to in_progress before spawning a tasker
   - For independent tasks: spawn taskers in parallel (single message, multiple tool calls)
   - After each batch: apply tracker updates serially, then re-check `bd ready`
   - For acceptance review tasks: spawn verifier, not tasker
   - "Follow beads-core/references/execution-orchestration.md for the full loop."

4. **Step 3: Closure**
   - After all implementation tasks close and acceptance review passes: close the epic
   - "See beads-core/references/execution-orchestration.md for closure order."

5. **Operational rules reference**
   - "Load beads-core/references/core-rules.md for serialized writes, agent delegation, and git safety rules."
   - Note: After resolving deduplication (task taj), keep only one reference per concern. If core-rules.md owns serialized writes, do not also point at execution-orchestration.md for the same rule.

### What NOT to include:
- Planning logic (that is beads-plan's job)
- Issue creation (that is beads-plan's job)
- Discussion or exploration handling

## Files to Modify
- `plugins/beads-tasks/skills/beads-work/SKILL.md` (full implementation replacing stub)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] File triages in_progress tasks before resuming (bd show, age check, blocker check)
- [ ] File describes user-confirmation before auto-resuming stale in_progress tasks (>24h or has blocker comment)
- [ ] File checks bd ready when no healthy in_progress tasks exist
- [ ] File says to move task to in_progress before spawning tasker
- [ ] File references execution-orchestration.md for the full loop
- [ ] File references core-rules.md for shared operational rules
- [ ] No planning or issue-creation content
- [ ] File is under 70 lines

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Implemented — 48-line SKILL.md replacing stub. In_progress triage (bd show, age check, blocker check), user confirmation before auto-resuming stale tasks, bd ready fallback, move-before-spawn rule, references execution-orchestration.md and core-rules.md. All 8 acceptance criteria pass.

title: task
description:
## Description
Complete rewrite of beads-plan/SKILL.md plus an update to planning.md. The new SKILL.md opens with the intake decision, stays planning-only, and delegates all workflow steps to beads-core references. The actual step-by-step planning workflow moves into planning.md as a named section.

## Instructions

### Part A — Update beads-core/references/planning.md
Add a clearly named **"Planning Workflow"** section to planning.md containing the step-by-step process (currently in beads-plan/SKILL.md lines 142-149):
1. Review the current conversation to understand what to build or fix
2. Confirm context is sufficient (via planning-intake.md — this is done before arriving here)
3. Choose epic+tasks or standalone tasks based on complexity (use Decision Framework from core-rules.md)
4. Create the epic and tasks — follow the issue structure rules in this file and beads-issue-workflow.md
5. Set dependencies with `bd dep add`
6. Present the plan to the user — brief summary of what will be built and in what order

Place this section near the top of planning.md, before the existing content on issue types and quality bar.

### Part B — Rewrite plugins/beads-tasks/skills/beads-plan/SKILL.md
Replace the entire content with this structure:

1. **Frontmatter**: name=beads-plan, user-invocable=true, disable-model-invocation=true. Description: "Create beads issues for the current conversation — determines what to plan, asks questions if context is missing, then creates epics, tasks, and acceptance-review tasks."

2. **Step 1 — Intake**: Load and follow `beads-core/references/planning-intake.md`. Either proceed (context is sufficient) or ask questions until it is. Do not proceed to Step 2 until there is enough to write a ticket.

3. **Step 2 — Plan**: Follow the **Planning Workflow** section of `beads-core/references/planning.md` to create the epics, tasks, and acceptance-review task. Load `beads-core/references/beads-issue-workflow.md` for label and dependency rules.

4. **Step 3 — Rules**: "For shared operational rules (serialized writes, agent delegation, git safety), see `beads-core/references/core-rules.md`."

### What NOT to include in the new SKILL.md:
- Any execution trigger content (bd ready, spawn taskers, in_progress)
- Any duplicated core rules — reference core-rules.md instead
- Any planning workflow steps — those are now in planning.md
- The four-use-case section

## Files to Modify
- `plugins/beads-tasks/skills/beads-core/references/planning.md` (add Planning Workflow section)
- `plugins/beads-tasks/skills/beads-plan/SKILL.md` (full rewrite)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] planning.md has a named "Planning Workflow" section with the 6 steps above
- [ ] beads-plan/SKILL.md opens with Step 1 (intake, references planning-intake.md)
- [ ] beads-plan/SKILL.md Step 2 says "follow the Planning Workflow section of planning.md"
- [ ] No execution trigger content in beads-plan/SKILL.md
- [ ] No duplicated core rules in beads-plan/SKILL.md
- [ ] beads-plan/SKILL.md is under 60 lines

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
Reviewer finding (Finding 6): the task instructs the new beads-plan/SKILL.md to 'Load planning.md and beads-issue-workflow.md and follow their instructions' but those reference files contain bash examples, not a step-by-step workflow tailored to the current conversation. If the skill is reduced to 'load X and Y, follow them', the workflow disappears — the 60-line target is achievable only by deleting workflow steps.

Two options: (a) keep the explicit planning process steps (current beads-plan/SKILL.md lines 142-149) inside the new SKILL.md and have those steps cite the reference files for detail; (b) move the planning workflow section into planning.md itself, and have SKILL.md say 'Step 2: follow the Planning Workflow section of beads-core/references/planning.md'.

comments.text: Decision recorded (Option B): Move the step-by-step planning workflow into beads-core/references/planning.md as a named 'Planning Workflow' section. beads-plan/SKILL.md will say 'follow the Planning Workflow section in beads-core/references/planning.md' — no workflow steps duplicated in the SKILL.md itself. Task scope includes updating planning.md to contain that workflow section (extracting from current beads-plan/SKILL.md lines 142-149).

comments.text: Implemented. planning.md has 'Planning Workflow' section (6 steps) near top; old 'Planning workflow' section renamed to 'Issue structure checklist' to avoid ambiguity. SKILL.md rewritten to 22 lines — 3 steps, no duplicated rules or execution content. All acceptance criteria pass.

title: task
description:
## Description
Create a dedicated reference file for the decision logic that beads-plan executes at startup: scan the conversation, decide whether there is enough context to plan, and either proceed or ask targeted questions. Invoking /beads-tasks:beads-plan IS the declaration of planning intent — no discussion/exploration branch is needed.

## Instructions
Create `plugins/beads-tasks/skills/beads-core/references/planning-intake.md` with the following structure:

### Section 1: Decision algorithm
Describe how to assess whether the current conversation has enough context to create beads issues:

**Sufficient context signals** (any of these → proceed to planning):
- A feature, improvement, or change the user wants to build — described with enough specificity to write a ticket
- A bug or problem they want fixed — with observable symptoms or reproduction steps
- An explicit scope ("rewrite X", "add Y to Z", "fix the issue where...")
- Answers to what-to-build AND what-done-looks-like already present in the conversation

**Insufficient context** (ask questions when these are missing):
- What to build or fix is vague or absent
- The desired outcome is unclear
- No way to write testable acceptance criteria yet

### Section 2: Intake questions (ask only what is missing)
When context is insufficient, ask only the questions whose answers are not already in the conversation:

1. What do you want to build or fix? (scope — required)
2. What is the desired outcome, or what problem does this solve? (required)
3. Are there known constraints, files, or systems involved? (optional — ask if scope is ambiguous)
4. How would you verify it works — what does "done" look like?

For question 4: reference the planner quality bar in `beads-core/references/planning.md` — the answer should satisfy the criteria listed there (testable acceptance criteria, no unresolved questions). Do NOT restate those criteria here.

### Section 3: When to stop asking and plan
Stop asking as soon as questions 1 and 2 are answered with enough specificity that a ticket description can be written. At that point, proceed immediately — do not ask further questions.

## Files to Modify
- `plugins/beads-tasks/skills/beads-core/references/planning-intake.md` (create)
- `plugins/beads-tasks/skills/beads-core/SKILL.md` (add routing entry: "Planning intake decision logic → references/planning-intake.md")

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] planning-intake.md exists with all three sections
- [ ] Decision algorithm lists concrete "sufficient" and "insufficient" signals
- [ ] Intake questions are ordered and scoped to what is missing (not a fixed list always asked)
- [ ] Question 4 references planning.md quality bar rather than restating it
- [ ] "When to stop asking" rule is stated explicitly
- [ ] No discussion/exploration branch — skill invocation implies planning intent
- [ ] beads-core/SKILL.md routing table has entry for planning-intake.md

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
Reviewer finding (Finding 5): planning-intake.md has no exit path for discussion/exploration. When a user invokes beads-plan to explore architecture or ask tradeoffs — without intent to create issues — the skill will still push them through intake questions.

Two options: (a) add a third branch to planning-intake.md: 'if user signals exploration intent, do not push planning — but still apply the beads-must-reflect-reality rule from core-rules.md if decisions emerge'; (b) accept that beads-plan is strictly planning-only and move discussion handling + beads-must-reflect-reality into core-rules.md so beads-work and agents inherit it.

Reviewer also flagged (Finding 10): the intake questions in Section 2 (what to build, constraints, done criteria) duplicate the planner quality bar in planning.md lines 13-24. One canonical location should own this checklist — either point Section 2 at planning.md's quality bar, or move it here and cross-reference from planning.md.

**Awaiting user decision on both before finalizing this task.**

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Decision recorded: /beads-tasks:beads-plan invocation IS the planning intent — no discussion/exploration branch needed. planning-intake.md has exactly two paths: (1) enough context in the conversation → proceed to planning, (2) not enough context → ask targeted questions until it is. The intake questions should reference planning.md's quality bar rather than restate it (resolves Finding 10 duplication concern).

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: planning-intake.md created with all three sections. Decision algorithm covers sufficient/insufficient signals. Intake questions reference planning.md quality bar for Q4. Stop-asking rule explicit. No exploration branch. SKILL.md routing table updated.

title: epic
description:
## Description
The beads-tasks plugin needs a clean separation of concerns and elimination of duplication:

1. **beads-plan is overloaded** — it handles planning, execution triggering, ad-hoc work, AND discussion. It should do one thing: take the current conversation and turn it into beads issues. The decision logic (does this conversation have planning context?) is buried at the bottom rather than up front.

2. **beads-work is a stub** — it exists as a placeholder with no content. It should own the execution loop: check in_progress tasks, or pick the next ready task, and run it.

3. **Shared rules are duplicated** — core rules (serialized writes, priority scheme, git safety, agent delegation, beads philosophy) live in beads-plan/SKILL.md and are not shared with beads-work or agents.

## Files Involved
- `plugins/beads-tasks/skills/beads-plan/SKILL.md` (rewrite)
- `plugins/beads-tasks/skills/beads-work/SKILL.md` (implement from stub)
- `plugins/beads-tasks/skills/beads-core/SKILL.md` (update routing)
- `plugins/beads-tasks/skills/beads-core/references/core-rules.md` (new)
- `plugins/beads-tasks/skills/beads-core/references/planning-intake.md` (new)

## Success Criteria
- [ ] beads-plan/SKILL.md opens with decision logic: does conversation have planning context? If yes, plan. If no, ask targeted questions.
- [ ] beads-plan/SKILL.md is scoped to planning only — no execution trigger, no ad-hoc work handling
- [ ] beads-work/SKILL.md implements the full execution loop (in_progress first, then bd ready, then taskers)
- [ ] All shared operational rules live in beads-core/references/core-rules.md
- [ ] beads-core/SKILL.md routing table covers all reference files including the two new ones
- [ ] Acceptance review confirms no duplication and correct behavior per skill

title: Research: evaluate new beads features and decide what to adopt in beads-tasks plugin
description:
## Description
A gap analysis identified 7 beads features that are available but not used in the plugin. This research task must evaluate each feature in the context of the plugin's AI-agent orchestration workflow and produce a concrete adopt/skip decision for each, with implementation tasks for anything worth adopting.

## Context: Features to evaluate

1. **`bd prime --hook-json`** — Replace the static `prime.md` SessionStart hook with a dynamic one. `bd prime` injects project memories, current in-progress state, and sync status. `bd remember` for cross-session project facts. Would eliminate the maintenance burden of keeping `prime.md` current.

2. **`bd update --claim`** — Atomic in_progress assignment (sets assignee + status in one write). Prevents race conditions in parallel-agent setups. Currently the orchestrator does a two-step non-atomic write.

3. **`gh:pr` and `gh:run` gate types** — Model PR-merge and CI-green waits as native gates instead of optimistically closing beads issues when a PR is only opened. Would prevent starting dependent work before code actually lands.

4. **`decision` issue type** — First-class searchable issue type for architectural and design decisions. Currently the plugin buries decisions in `bd comments add`. A `decision` issue is cross-linkable and survives context compaction.

5. **`bd search` before filing bugs** — Add a duplicate-prevention step to the tasker and orchestrator before creating any new bug. Missing today.

6. **Execution metadata on tickets** — Planner can embed `execution_suggested_model`, `execution_agent_type`, etc. in ticket metadata at creation time. Would let tickets self-describe routing instead of hardcoding it in agent files.

7. **`bd stale` at session start** — Surface abandoned in-progress issues from sessions where agents lost context. Missing from the session-open checklist.

## Instructions
1. For each feature: evaluate whether it genuinely improves the plugin's AI-agent workflow or adds unnecessary complexity.
2. For features worth adopting: define exactly how they would change the plugin (which files, which instructions, what new behavior).
3. For features to skip: give a one-line reason.
4. For each "adopt" decision: create a concrete, executable beads implementation task with instructions, file targets, and testable acceptance criteria.
5. Consider whether any features interact or should be adopted together.
6. Do NOT implement any changes — output only the implementation tasks.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Adopt/skip decision documented for each of the 7 features
- [ ] Each "adopt" decision has a concrete implementation task
- [ ] No implementation tasks require guessing — all have file targets and specific instructions
- [ ] Features with interactions are noted and sequenced correctly
close_reason: Superseded by claude-dev-tools-hru. The concrete refactor plan (separate beads-plan/beads-work, consolidate into beads-core) makes this research task unnecessary — decisions are already encoded in the implementation tasks.

title: Research: resolve the 5 conflicts in beads-tasks plugin and design single-source-of-truth structure
description:
## Description
A review identified 5 concrete conflicts across `prime.md`, `coder-beads/SKILL.md`, and the three agent files. This research task must analyze each conflict, decide the authoritative location and formulation, and output a set of concrete implementation tasks to execute the cleanup.

## Context: The 5 conflicts

1. **`coder-beads` skill is written for orchestrators but subagents are told to load it** — `tasker.md`, `reviewer.md`, `verifier.md` all instruct agents to load `coder-beads`, but the skill's content (`SKILL.md`) is authored exclusively from the orchestrator's perspective. A tasker loading it receives conflicting role instructions.

2. **Verifier's delegation entry differs between files** — `prime.md` scopes the verifier to any "verification needed" situation and says it returns "recommended tracker actions"; `SKILL.md` is narrower and omits tracker-action output from the verifier's return contract.

3. **No-gate-type constraint only in `prime.md`** — The "do NOT use native beads `gate` type" rule is stated twice in `prime.md` but absent from `verifier.md` (which owns acceptance review execution) and from `SKILL.md`.

4. **`/plan` mode instruction conflict** — `SKILL.md` mandates entering `/plan` mode for any discussion or planning. `prime.md` describes a "Discussion / Exploration" use case that explicitly does NOT push into planning structure prematurely, and never mentions `/plan`.

5. **Verifier can/cannot close issues directly** — The `verifier.md` closure table says "YES — Close with evidence" but the "What You Do NOT Do" section and all scope descriptions say the verifier returns "recommended close reason to the caller" and must NOT modify tracker state directly.

Additionally, `prime.md` and `SKILL.md` share near-verbatim content for: beads-is-the-tracker rule, serialized writes rule, issue-before-execution, priority-is-numeric, beads-must-reflect-reality, subagent delegation table, parallel execution + after-agents protocol, and session close protocol.

## Instructions
1. For each of the 5 conflicts: decide the single authoritative location and the correct formulation.
2. For the prime.md / SKILL.md duplication: decide what belongs in each file and what should be referenced rather than repeated.
3. For the subagent files: identify which overlapping content (tracker comment discipline, bug discovery, ticket readiness checks) can be consolidated and where.
4. For each decision: create a concrete, executable beads implementation task with instructions, file targets, and testable acceptance criteria.
5. Do NOT implement any changes — output only the implementation tasks.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Each of the 5 conflicts has a documented resolution decision
- [ ] The prime.md / SKILL.md duplication boundary is clearly defined
- [ ] Each decision maps to one or more concrete beads implementation tasks
- [ ] No implementation tasks require guessing — all have file targets and specific instructions
close_reason: Superseded by claude-dev-tools-hru. The 5 conflicts are addressed directly by the refactor tasks rather than through a separate research phase.

title: beads-tasks plugin: cleanup contradictions and adopt new beads features
description:

title: Add internal-consistency validation: section-level cross-refs and version mirrors
description:
## Description

Two recent slips share the same root cause: internal references and version mirrors drift silently because nothing validates them.

1. `planning.md:9` references "the Decision Framework in core-rules.md" — section does not exist (broken since commit 3705170, 3 releases ago).
2. `marketplace.json` plugin versions out of sync with `plugin.json` versions for all 5 plugins, undetected for 3 releases.

Both classes of bug were caught manually during release validation, not by any automated check. `plugin-dev:plugin-validator` only checks file-level markdown links, not section-level references in prose, and the release process only bumps `plugin.json`.

## Instructions

1. Add a validation step that scans markdown files for prose references like "the X section in Y.md" and confirms section X exists in Y.md (or warn if such a section reference can't be resolved). Either extend `plugin-dev:plugin-validator` or add a standalone script.
2. Add a validation step that compares each `plugins/*/.claude-plugin/plugin.json` version against the matching entry in `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json`, failing if any mismatch.
3. Wire both checks into the release process (update `docs/RELEASING.md` to require running them before tagging) and into local plugin-validator runs.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Running the validation against current `main` after fixing bd-12 and the Decision Framework bug returns clean
- [ ] Deliberately desyncing one plugin.json version produces a failing validation
- [ ] Deliberately adding a "reference the Foo section in bar.md" pointer to a nonexistent section produces a failing validation
- [ ] `docs/RELEASING.md` references the new check as a required pre-release step

title: Acceptance Review: keep-awake-linux plugin
description:
## Description

Gate task for closing epic claude-dev-tools-yt7. Runs after all child implementation tasks (claude-dev-tools-0vo, claude-dev-tools-8ed, claude-dev-tools-guz, claude-dev-tools-8ry, claude-dev-tools-h97) are closed. Verifies the plugin actually works end-to-end on a real Linux desktop with at least two parallel Claude Code sessions.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All child tasks closed (claude-dev-tools-0vo, claude-dev-tools-8ed, claude-dev-tools-guz, claude-dev-tools-8ry, claude-dev-tools-h97).

- [ ] No manual `~/.claude/settings.json` edits required to enable hooks.
- [ ] Scenario A (single session): submit a prompt → `systemd-inhibit --list` shows the inhibitor within ~1s → turn ends → inhibitor released within ~1s.
- [ ] Scenario B (parallel sessions): both busy → 1 inhibitor, 2 markers; one finishes → 1 inhibitor, 1 marker; both finish → released.
- [ ] Scenario C (Notification): trigger a permission prompt → status shows released while Claude waits on user.
- [ ] Scenario D (crash recovery): `kill -9` a Claude session mid-turn → its marker is stale → next `SessionStart` (or `keep-awake gc`) removes it → inhibitor releases if it was the only one.
- [ ] Scenario E (manual): `/keep-awake on` holds inhibitor independent of Claude state; `/keep-awake off` releases.
- [ ] Scenario F (non-systemd fallback): with `PATH=/nowhere keep-awake start probe`, no error, no inhibitor, stderr warning logged.
- [ ] Scenario G (concurrency): 5 parallel `start` calls produce exactly 1 inhibitor process.
- [ ] README shell snippets all execute successfully.
- [ ] Defects discovered during review filed as new beads issues (do NOT silently fix).
- [ ] Closing recommendation (close-as-completed or block on followups) recorded as a comment on this task.

## Verification approach

Run the verifier agent. It must:
1. Install the plugin in a fresh shell.
2. Execute every scenario above and record actual vs expected.
3. Cross-check the README snippets.
4. File new beads issues for any deviation.
5. Post the close recommendation as a comment on this task.

title: Wire hooks/hooks.json: SessionStart, UserPromptSubmit, Stop, Notification, SessionEnd
description:
## Description

Create `hooks/hooks.json` that auto-installs the hook wiring when the plugin is installed. Each hook reads `session_id` from stdin JSON and dispatches to `bin/keep-awake`.

Parent epic: claude-dev-tools-yt7. Depends on claude-dev-tools-8ed (helper script).

## File to create

`plugins/keep-awake-linux/hooks/hooks.json`

## Hook → action mapping (agreed during planning)

| Hook event | Command | Why |
|---|---|---|
| `SessionStart` | `keep-awake gc` | Reap markers left by prior crashed sessions (SessionEnd isn't guaranteed on Ctrl-C/kill). |
| `UserPromptSubmit` | `keep-awake start <session_id>` | User just sent a prompt — Claude is now busy. |
| `Stop` | `keep-awake stop <session_id>` | Claude finished a turn — idle, waiting for user. |
| `Notification` | `keep-awake stop <session_id>` | Claude paused for user input (e.g. permission prompt). Release so power isn't wasted and the user notices when they return. |
| `SessionEnd` | `keep-awake stop <session_id>` | Final cleanup on clean exit. |

**Notification = release** decision rationale (from user): if Claude is blocked waiting on the user and the user is away, sleeping is fine and arguably preferable — it conserves power and ensures the user notices the prompt when they return to a re-awakened machine.

## Implementation notes

- Each hook command is a small bash one-liner that parses `session_id` from the stdin JSON. Recommended pattern (avoid requiring `jq` on the user's host — fall back gracefully):
  ```bash
  SID=$(jq -r '.session_id // empty' 2>/dev/null) || SID=""
  [ -n "$SID" ] && keep-awake start "$SID" || true
  ```
- Hooks must NEVER exit non-zero — that would block Claude. Use `|| true`.
- Hooks must be fast (<100ms typical). The helper is already fast (just file ops + maybe one spawn).
- Use `"matcher": "*"` for all events (no tool filtering needed here).
- The `bin/` directory of an installed plugin is on `$PATH` during hook execution, so `keep-awake` resolves without an absolute path.

## hooks.json shape (reference)

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "SessionStart": [
      { "matcher": "*", "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "keep-awake gc || true" }] }
    ],
    "UserPromptSubmit": [
      { "matcher": "*", "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "SID=$(jq -r '.session_id // empty' 2>/dev/null || true); [ -n \"$SID\" ] && keep-awake start \"$SID\" || true" }] }
    ],
    "Stop": [
      { "matcher": "*", "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "SID=$(jq -r '.session_id // empty' 2>/dev/null || true); [ -n \"$SID\" ] && keep-awake stop \"$SID\" || true" }] }
    ],
    "Notification": [
      { "matcher": "*", "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "SID=$(jq -r '.session_id // empty' 2>/dev/null || true); [ -n \"$SID\" ] && keep-awake stop \"$SID\" || true" }] }
    ],
    "SessionEnd": [
      { "matcher": "*", "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "SID=$(jq -r '.session_id // empty' 2>/dev/null || true); [ -n \"$SID\" ] && keep-awake stop \"$SID\" || true" }] }
    ]
  }
}
```

If `jq` is too heavy a dep to assume, replace the parser with a `sed`/`grep`-only extraction. Decide during implementation; document the choice in the README (task claude-dev-tools-T5).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `hooks/hooks.json` is valid JSON (`jq . hooks/hooks.json`).
- [ ] All five events (`SessionStart`, `UserPromptSubmit`, `Stop`, `Notification`, `SessionEnd`) are wired.
- [ ] Each hook command exits 0 even when given empty/garbage stdin.
- [ ] After `/plugin install`, hook events fire as expected against a real Claude session.

## Verification (how to test)

```bash
# 1) JSON validity
jq . plugins/keep-awake-linux/hooks/hooks.json

# 2) Each hook command tolerates empty / bad input
echo '{}' | sh -c 'SID=$(jq -r ".session_id // empty" 2>/dev/null || true); [ -n "$SID" ] && keep-awake start "$SID" || true'
echo 'not json' | sh -c 'SID=$(jq -r ".session_id // empty" 2>/dev/null || true); [ -n "$SID" ] && keep-awake start "$SID" || true'
# expect: exit 0, no error

# 3) Each hook fires the right verb against a synthetic session_id
echo '{"session_id":"test-123"}' | sh -c 'SID=$(jq -r ".session_id // empty"); keep-awake start "$SID"'
keep-awake status                              # expect: session "test-123" listed
echo '{"session_id":"test-123"}' | sh -c 'SID=$(jq -r ".session_id // empty"); keep-awake stop "$SID"'
keep-awake status                              # expect: released

# 4) End-to-end with a real session (manual)
/plugin install keep-awake-linux@claude-dev-tools
# Start a new claude session; in another shell:
watch -n1 'keep-awake status; echo ---; systemd-inhibit --list | grep claude-keep-awake'
# In the claude session: submit a prompt. Status flips to busy + inhibitor appears.
# When the turn finishes (Stop fires), status flips to released within ~1s.

# 5) Multi-session sanity
# Start TWO claude sessions in parallel kitty windows. Submit prompts in both.
# Status shows 2 sessions, 1 inhibitor. Stop one — still 1 session, 1 inhibitor. Stop both — released.

# 6) Notification path
# Trigger a permission prompt (e.g. a tool Claude needs to call that prompts).
# Verify: while waiting on you, status shows "released" (Notification fired stop).
```

title: Implement bin/keep-awake helper script (start|stop|status|gc|force-on|force-off)
description:
## Description

Implement the core bash helper that manages per-session busy markers and the lifetime of the `systemd-inhibit` subprocess. This is the only stateful piece — hooks (task claude-dev-tools-T3) and the slash command (task claude-dev-tools-T4) are thin shims that call into this.

Parent epic: claude-dev-tools-yt7. Depends on claude-dev-tools-0vo (scaffold).

## File to create

`plugins/keep-awake-linux/bin/keep-awake` (executable bash, `chmod +x`)

## State layout

```
$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/claude-keep-awake/
├── inhibitor.pid       # PID of the running systemd-inhibit subprocess (absent if none)
└── sessions/
    ├── <session_id>    # one file per busy session, contents: $PPID of the hook caller
    └── manual          # marker for `force-on` (slash command or CLI usage)
```

Fallback when `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` is unset: use `/tmp/claude-keep-awake-$(id -u)/` with `0700` mode.

## Verbs

| Verb | Args | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `start` | `<session_id>` | Create `sessions/<session_id>` with PPID inside. If no inhibitor running, spawn one and record its PID. Idempotent. |
| `stop` | `<session_id>` | Remove `sessions/<session_id>`. If `sessions/` is now empty, kill the inhibitor PID and remove `inhibitor.pid`. Idempotent (no error if marker missing). |
| `status` | (none) | Print: inhibitor PID (or "released"), count + list of active session markers, last spawn/release timestamps. Exit 0. |
| `gc` | (none) | For each marker file, read its PPID; if that PID no longer exists (`kill -0` fails), remove the marker. Then reconcile inhibitor: if zero markers but inhibitor running → kill it; if markers exist but no inhibitor → spawn one. |
| `force-on` | (none) | Equivalent to `start manual`. |
| `force-off` | (none) | Equivalent to `stop manual`. |

## Spawn command

```bash
systemd-inhibit \
  --what=idle:sleep \
  --who="claude-keep-awake" \
  --why="Claude Code is actively working" \
  --mode=block \
  sleep infinity &
echo $! > "$STATE_DIR/inhibitor.pid"
```

Use `setsid` or `nohup` + `disown` so the inhibitor survives the hook process exit.

## Graceful fallback

- If `command -v systemd-inhibit` returns non-zero, log one line to stderr (`keep-awake: systemd-inhibit not available, no-op`) and exit 0. All verbs become no-ops. The plugin must NOT break Claude Code on non-systemd hosts.
- Same fallback if running inside a container without access to the host's systemd-logind.

## Concurrency

Multiple hooks fire simultaneously (especially in a multi-session setup). Wrap the state-mutating critical sections in `flock` on `$STATE_DIR/.lock`:

```bash
exec 9>"$STATE_DIR/.lock"
flock 9
# … mutate marker set, then read final size to decide spawn/kill …
```

## Implementation outline

1. Resolve `STATE_DIR` (with `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` fallback).
2. Detect systemd-inhibit availability; if absent, exit 0.
3. Acquire flock.
4. Dispatch on `$1`.
5. After mutating markers, reconcile: count `sessions/*`; ensure inhibitor matches (spawn if needed, kill if not).
6. On `gc`, also strip dead-PID markers before reconciling.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Script is executable and runs `keep-awake status` with no errors on a fresh state dir.
- [ ] `keep-awake start abc` then `systemd-inhibit --list` shows a `claude-keep-awake` entry blocking `idle:sleep`.
- [ ] `keep-awake start xyz` (second session) does NOT spawn a second inhibitor — count stays at 1.
- [ ] `keep-awake stop abc` while `xyz` is still busy keeps the inhibitor alive.
- [ ] `keep-awake stop xyz` (last session) kills the inhibitor; `systemd-inhibit --list` no longer shows it.
- [ ] `keep-awake gc` with a marker whose PID is dead removes that marker.
- [ ] Concurrent `start` calls (e.g. `for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do keep-awake start s$i & done; wait`) produce exactly one inhibitor process and 5 markers.
- [ ] On a host where `systemd-inhibit` is uninstalled, the script exits 0 with a stderr warning and creates no inhibitor.

## Verification (how to test)

```bash
# Setup
PLUGIN_DIR="plugins/keep-awake-linux"
KA="$PLUGIN_DIR/bin/keep-awake"
chmod +x "$KA"

# 1) Cold start
"$KA" status
# expect: inhibitor released, 0 sessions

# 2) Single-session lifecycle
"$KA" start sess-a
systemd-inhibit --list | grep claude-keep-awake   # expect: 1 entry, what=idle:sleep
"$KA" status                                       # expect: 1 session, inhibitor PID
"$KA" stop sess-a
systemd-inhibit --list | grep claude-keep-awake   # expect: no match
"$KA" status                                       # expect: released, 0 sessions

# 3) Multi-session
"$KA" start sess-a
"$KA" start sess-b
PID1=$(cat "${XDG_RUNTIME_DIR:-/tmp/claude-keep-awake-$(id -u)}/inhibitor.pid")
"$KA" stop sess-a
PID2=$(cat "${XDG_RUNTIME_DIR:-/tmp/claude-keep-awake-$(id -u)}/inhibitor.pid")
[ "$PID1" = "$PID2" ] && echo OK || echo FAIL   # expect OK (inhibitor preserved)
"$KA" stop sess-b
"$KA" status                                       # expect: released

# 4) GC of dead sessions
"$KA" start sess-zombie
# manually corrupt marker to a fake PID
echo 99999 > "${XDG_RUNTIME_DIR:-/tmp/claude-keep-awake-$(id -u)}/sessions/sess-zombie"
"$KA" gc
"$KA" status                                       # expect: 0 sessions, released

# 5) Concurrency
for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do "$KA" start "s$i" & done; wait
"$KA" status                                       # expect: exactly 5 sessions, 1 inhibitor PID
pgrep -af 'systemd-inhibit.*claude-keep-awake' | wc -l   # expect: 1
for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do "$KA" stop "s$i"; done
"$KA" status                                       # expect: released

# 6) Non-systemd fallback (simulate)
PATH="/usr/bin:/bin" sh -c 'command -v systemd-inhibit'   # confirm presence on host
# On a host genuinely missing systemd-inhibit (or via mocked PATH):
PATH=/nowhere "$KA" start probe
# expect: stderr warning, exit 0, no state created

# 7) Manual force-on
"$KA" force-on
"$KA" status                                       # expect: 1 session named "manual"
"$KA" force-off
"$KA" status                                       # expect: released
```

title: Linux keep-awake plugin: inhibit system sleep while Claude is actively working
description:
## Description

Build a new Claude Code plugin for the `claude-dev-tools` marketplace that prevents the host Linux system from going to sleep / idle while any Claude Code session is actively processing a turn, and releases the inhibitor automatically when all sessions are idle (waiting for the user) or have ended.

Native Linux only. Uses `systemd-inhibit`. No Electron, no tray icon, no background daemon.

## Motivation

Long-running Claude sessions on Linux desktops get silently paused when the system suspends. macOS users have several plugins for this (caffeinate-based), but **no native-Linux plugin exists**. The closest cross-platform option (`cc-caffeine`) requires running a headless Electron server with undocumented/experimental Linux support — too heavyweight for a desktop tool.

User runs many Claude sessions in parallel. A single inhibitor process that reacts to per-session hook events is the right model: stays awake while *any* session is busy, releases when *all* are idle.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] New plugin lives at `plugins/<name>/` and is registered in `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json`.
- [ ] Plugin installs cleanly via `/plugin install` and auto-wires its hooks (no manual `settings.json` edits).
- [ ] On `UserPromptSubmit`, the system stops being eligible for idle/sleep (verified with `systemd-inhibit --list`).
- [ ] On `Stop` / `Notification` / `SessionEnd`, when all known sessions are idle, the inhibitor is released.
- [ ] Multiple parallel Claude sessions are tracked independently — system stays awake while ANY is busy.
- [ ] PID-based garbage collection on `SessionStart` cleans up markers from crashed/killed prior sessions.
- [ ] A `/keep-awake` slash command exists for manual force-on / force-off / status (so the user can hold the machine awake for non-Claude work too).
- [ ] On non-systemd hosts (or `systemd-inhibit` missing), the plugin no-ops gracefully with a one-line warning, not an error.
- [ ] README documents: what it does, install command, the hook events it uses, the marker-file location, how to inspect state, and the Notification = release decision.
- [ ] Acceptance Review task closed.

## Design (agreed during planning session)

**Mechanism**: A single `systemd-inhibit --what=idle:sleep --who=claude-keep-awake --why="Claude Code is working" --mode=block sleep infinity` subprocess. Spawned when the busy-session count goes 0 → 1, killed when it goes N → 0. PID stored in a runtime file.

**Per-session tracking**: Marker files in `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/claude-keep-awake/sessions/<session_id>` (fallback `/tmp/claude-keep-awake-$UID/`). Each marker stores the session's PID so stale markers can be GC'd when their owning process is dead.

**Hook → action map**:
| Hook event | Action |
|---|---|
| `SessionStart` | `keep-awake gc` (reap markers whose PID is dead) |
| `UserPromptSubmit` | `keep-awake start <session_id>` (mark busy) |
| `Stop` | `keep-awake stop <session_id>` (mark idle) |
| `Notification` | `keep-awake stop <session_id>` (Claude paused for user — release) |
| `SessionEnd` | `keep-awake stop <session_id>` (final cleanup) |

**Notification decision** (confirmed by user): release inhibit. Rationale: if Claude needs the user (permission prompt, etc.) and the user is away, sleeping the machine is fine — saves power and ensures the user notices when they return. Keeping awake while paused-for-user wastes power for no reason.

**Why no daemon**: hook events fire at the exact transitions we care about. A polling daemon would reinvent what hooks tell us synchronously.

**Why session_id markers (not a counter)**: counters get out of sync on crashes. File-per-session lets `gc` repair state by checking PID liveness; it also makes `status` a simple `ls`.

## Plugin manifest mechanics (verified during research)

- Plugins can ship `hooks/hooks.json` that **activates automatically** on `/plugin install` — no user-side `settings.json` edits required.
- Plugins can ship helper scripts in `bin/`; `bin/` is added to `$PATH` during hook execution.
- Hooks receive event metadata via **stdin JSON** including `session_id`, `transcript_path`, `cwd`, `hook_event_name`, and event-specific fields.
- Hook events confirmed present as of May 2026: `SessionStart`, `SessionEnd`, `UserPromptSubmit`, `Stop`, `StopFailure`, `PreToolUse`, `PostToolUse`, `Notification`.
- Caveat: `SessionEnd` is **not guaranteed** to fire on unclean exits (Ctrl-C, kill, OOM). That is why `SessionStart`-driven garbage collection is required.

## Suggested plugin name

`keep-awake-linux` (or `linux-keep-awake`). Implementer may rename — note that the repo convention is kebab-case (`claude-catppuccin`, `complexity-review`, `github-releases`). Adjust marketplace.json and homepage/repo fields to match the existing pattern.

## Prior art / sources

Researched May 2026, all macOS-first; no native-Linux equivalent:

- **cc-caffeine** — https://github.com/samber/cc-caffeine — claims cross-platform; Linux uses Electron's power-management API, requires a headless Electron server; install: `/plugin marketplace add samber/cc` then `/plugin install cc-caffeine@samber`. Reference: their hook wiring (UserPromptSubmit, PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Notification, Stop, SessionEnd) is a good model.
- **wakey-wakey** — https://github.com/alecf/claude-skills/blob/main/plugins/wakey-wakey/README.md — macOS only, `caffeinate -u`.
- **hiroro caffeinate plugin** — https://zenn.dev/hiropon22/articles/05215fbb614505?locale=en (repo: github.com/hiroro-work/claude-plugins) — macOS only; small and readable, good architectural reference for `plugin.json` + `hooks/hooks.json` + slash commands.
- **CharlonTank sleep-preventer** — https://github.com/CharlonTank/claude-code-sleep-preventer — macOS only, Rust + brew.
- **tngranados blog walkthrough** — https://tngranados.com/blog/preventing-mac-sleep-claude-code/ — the original DIY hook pattern (macOS). Translates 1:1: swap `caffeinate -i` for `systemd-inhibit --what=idle:sleep --mode=block sleep infinity &`.
- **Built-in macOS caffeinate behavior** — Claude Code spawns `caffeinate -i -t 300` on macOS only; open issue requesting opt-out: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/21432. No Linux equivalent built in — that is the gap this plugin fills.

## Official Claude Code docs to consult

- Hooks reference: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks
- Plugins guide: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins
- Plugins reference (manifest format, hooks.json shape, bin/ on PATH): https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins-reference

## Out of scope (explicitly)

- macOS / Windows support. Claude Code already handles macOS; Windows is a separate problem (PowerToys Keep Awake / SetThreadExecutionState).
- A tray icon, GUI, or system service.
- Replacing Claude Code's built-in macOS caffeinate behavior.
- An always-on mode masking systemd targets (`systemctl mask sleep.target …`) — explicitly avoided because it requires sudo, persists across reboots, and is easy to forget.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Addendum: End-to-end test plan

(Added per user request: "what you should also write is how to test this thing".)

Each implementation task carries its own "Verification" section with concrete shell commands. The acceptance-review task (claude-dev-tools-73f) ties them together into seven full scenarios. The composite plan below is the canonical reference for verifying this plugin actually does what it claims on a real Linux desktop with multiple parallel Claude Code sessions.

### Pre-flight

```bash
command -v systemd-inhibit                       # must exist
command -v jq                                    # used by hook stdin parser (or document a fallback)
ls "${XDG_RUNTIME_DIR:-/tmp/claude-keep-awake-$(id -u)}/"
```

### Scenario A — Single session, happy path

1. Install: `/plugin install keep-awake-linux@claude-dev-tools`.
2. Watch: `watch -n0.5 'keep-awake status; echo; systemd-inhibit --list | grep claude-keep-awake'`.
3. In a Claude session submit any prompt.
4. Expect: within ~1s the watcher shows 1 session marker AND `systemd-inhibit --list` shows a `claude-keep-awake` entry blocking `idle:sleep`.
5. Wait for the turn to finish.
6. Expect: within ~1s after `Stop` fires, marker is gone, `systemd-inhibit --list` no longer contains the entry.

### Scenario B — Parallel sessions

1. Start TWO Claude sessions in separate kitty windows.
2. Submit a long-running prompt in both.
3. Expect: `keep-awake status` shows 2 sessions, 1 inhibitor PID. `pgrep -af 'systemd-inhibit.*claude-keep-awake' | wc -l` = 1.
4. Wait for ONE session's turn to finish.
5. Expect: 1 session, still 1 inhibitor (unchanged PID).
6. Wait for the second to finish.
7. Expect: 0 sessions, inhibitor released.

### Scenario C — Notification = release

1. In a Claude session, trigger a permission prompt.
2. Expect: while Claude is paused waiting for you, `keep-awake status` shows 0 sessions; `systemd-inhibit --list` does not show the entry.
3. Approve the prompt; Claude resumes.
4. Expect: on the next `UserPromptSubmit`, status flips back to busy.

(If this scenario surprises us, re-evaluate the Notification = release decision and file a discussion task. Notification semantics in Claude Code are subtle.)

### Scenario D — Crash recovery (PID GC)

1. Start a Claude session, submit a long prompt.
2. Expect: 1 session, 1 inhibitor.
3. From another shell: `pkill -9 -f 'claude'`.
4. Expect: marker file still exists (SessionEnd didn't fire), inhibitor still running.
5. Start a new Claude session.
6. Expect: on `SessionStart`, `keep-awake gc` removes the stale marker; inhibitor releases.
7. Alternatively, run `keep-awake gc` manually.

### Scenario E — Manual `/keep-awake`

```
/keep-awake status     ->  released, 0 sessions
/keep-awake on         ->  marker "manual" added, inhibitor spawned
/keep-awake status     ->  1 session (manual), inhibitor PID
/keep-awake off        ->  marker removed, inhibitor released
```

Also: `/keep-awake on` while a real Claude turn is in flight must NOT spawn a second inhibitor.

### Scenario F — Non-systemd / missing-binary fallback

```bash
PATH=/nowhere keep-awake start probe
# expect: stderr "keep-awake: systemd-inhibit not available, no-op", exit 0, no state created
```

### Scenario G — Concurrency

```bash
for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do keep-awake start "s$i" & done; wait
pgrep -af 'systemd-inhibit.*claude-keep-awake' | wc -l   # expect 1
keep-awake status                                          # expect 5 sessions
for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do keep-awake stop "s$i"; done
keep-awake status                                          # expect released
```

### What "passing" means

Every scenario above must produce the expected output exactly. Deviations file new beads bugs (depending on claude-dev-tools-yt7). The acceptance review (claude-dev-tools-73f) only closes when all scenarios pass and no open defects remain against the plugin.

title: project-setup.md CLAUDE.md rule contradicts docs-init.md and docs-update.md
description:
File: plugins/project-docs/skills/project-docs/references/project-setup.md
Section: File ownership boundaries > CLAUDE.md
Problem: project-setup.md states CLAUDE.md "MUST NOT CONTAIN ANYTHING ELSE. All instructions always go to AGENTS.md." This directly contradicts docs-init.md (step 5) and docs-update.md (step 2), which both explicitly say: "Exists without @AGENTS.md: prepend @AGENTS.md + blank line; preserve all existing content unchanged below. Never overwrite custom content." The workflow entrypoints allow CLAUDE.md to carry additional content; the ownership doc forbids it. Any agent following project-setup.md would destroy existing CLAUDE.md content that docs-init.md and docs-update.md explicitly protect.
Fix: Align project-setup.md with the workflow docs. Change "MUST NOT CONTAIN ANYTHING ELSE. All instructions always go to AGENTS.md." to something like: "CLAUDE.md should start with @AGENTS.md as its first non-empty line. Existing content below @AGENTS.md should be preserved (never overwrite custom content)."
Found by: claude-dev-tools-sei.11

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
Verdict: REAL contradiction.

Root cause: project-setup.md line 7 says CLAUDE.md 'contains only `@AGENTS.md`' and lines 32-34 say 'MUST NOT CONTAIN ANYTHING ELSE'. But docs-init.md step 5 and docs-update.md step 2 both say 'preserve all existing content unchanged below. Never overwrite custom content.' These are mutually exclusive — an agent obeying project-setup.md would destroy content that the workflow docs guarantee will be preserved.

The lenient rule (preserve content below @AGENTS.md) is correct: project-docs-lifecycle.md only requires @AGENTS.md as the first line, examples/CLAUDE.md is ambiguous, and this repo's own CLAUDE.md contains Beads and Session Completion sections below @AGENTS.md.

Fix instructions — edit project-setup.md ONLY (docs-init.md and docs-update.md are correct):
File: plugins/project-docs/skills/project-docs/references/project-setup.md

Change 1 — line 7 (Baseline model):
  From: '`CLAUDE.md`: Claude Code entrypoint — contains only `@AGENTS.md`'
  To:   '`CLAUDE.md`: Claude Code entrypoint — starts with `@AGENTS.md` as the first non-empty line; existing content below is preserved'

Change 2 — lines 32-34 (File ownership boundaries → CLAUDE.md):
  From:
    - Claude Code entrypoint: `@AGENTS.md` as first line
    - MUST NOT CONTAIN ANYTHING ELSE. All instructions always go to `AGENTS.md`.
  To:
    - `@AGENTS.md` MUST be the first non-empty line
    - New instructions/routing always go to `AGENTS.md`, never directly to `CLAUDE.md`
    - Existing content below `@AGENTS.md` is preserved (never overwrite custom content); see docs-init.md / docs-update.md for the exact procedure

title: improve-doc SKILL.md path names don't match docs-improve.md mode names
description:
File: plugins/project-docs/skills/improve-doc/SKILL.md
Section: main body (execution path description)
Problem: SKILL.md describes its two execution paths as 'targeted single-doc path' and 'discussion-first analysis'. However, docs-improve.md defines its two execution modes as 'discussion-first mode' and 'incident-driven mode'. The term 'targeted single-doc path' does not appear in docs-improve.md at all. The actual mode is 'incident-driven mode', which is triggered by a concrete docs failure/incident — not merely by supplying a specific doc file and a concrete problem to fix. This mismatch means the delegation instructions in SKILL.md will mislead the agent: it will run the targeted single-doc path when the user provides any file+problem combination, but docs-improve.md's incident-driven mode is scoped to failure/incident scenarios and still follows a multi-step confirmation workflow, not a direct bypass.
Fix: Replace 'targeted single-doc path' with 'incident-driven mode' (matching docs-improve.md terminology), and align the trigger condition to match: 'if the user provides a concrete docs failure or incident context' rather than 'if the user supplies a specific doc file and a concrete problem to fix'.
Found by: claude-dev-tools-sei.13

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
Verdict: REAL.

Root cause: SKILL.md (lines 16-17) uses invented terminology ('targeted single-doc path') not defined anywhere in docs-improve.md or project-docs-lifecycle.md. The canonical mode names are 'discussion-first mode' and 'incident-driven mode'. The trigger conditions are also materially different — SKILL.md fires the non-discussion path on any 'specific doc file + concrete problem' but incident-driven mode requires a concrete docs failure/incident. Even when the right mode is selected, SKILL.md says to run it 'directly' which implies skipping confirmation, while docs-improve.md incident-driven mode still has a 3-step workflow with 'confirm high-impact changes, then implement and verify'.

Fix instructions:
File: plugins/project-docs/skills/improve-doc/SKILL.md
Lines 16-17

Replace:
  '- If the user supplies a specific doc file and a concrete problem to fix, run the targeted single-doc path directly.'
  '- Otherwise, run discussion-first analysis (positives, negatives, proposals), ask what to improve before edits, and require explicit confirmation before aggressive removals, consolidation, or deletion.'

With:
  '- If the user provides a concrete docs failure or incident context, run incident-driven mode (capture incident, propose recurrence-prevention edits, confirm high-impact changes, then implement and verify).'
  '- Otherwise, run discussion-first mode (summarize strengths, gaps, and options; ask which options to apply; then implement with verification).'

Reason: uses canonical mode names matching docs-improve.md and project-docs-lifecycle.md:118; tightens the incident trigger to match docs-improve.md; removes 'directly' which falsely implies a bypass of the confirmation step.

title: create-docs SKILL.md uses 'create-docs flow' name not matching core skill routing table
description:

A reader following this instruction must infer which of the 4 flows to run rather than finding an exact match.

Found by: claude-dev-tools-sei.12

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
Verdict: NOT REAL as filed — recommend close or rescope.

Analysis: The name mismatch ('create-docs flow' vs routing table label 'Create project docs') is not an ambiguity in practice. The wrapper skill is literally named 'create-docs', there is only one create flow in the routing table, and the intent is unambiguous. No agent or user would mis-route because of this phrasing.

Broader finding: the same loose pattern exists across all 4 wrapper skills — none use the exact routing-table label with a row reference. This is a consistent style choice, not a per-wrapper defect.

Recommended action: Close this bug as won't-fix. If consistent labeling across all 4 wrapper skills is desired, open a single standardization chore covering: create-docs, review-docs, improve-doc, and init-or-update-docs — aligning each wrapper's delegation instruction to use the exact routing-table label. Filing one wrapper in isolation leaves the inconsistency in the other three.

title: verifier.md contradicts beads-acceptance-review.md on who closes the acceptance task

Found by: claude-dev-tools-sei.3

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
Verdict: REAL. The authoritative pattern ('subagents recommend, orchestrator writes') is established in core-rules.md and followed by every agent definition — beads-acceptance-review.md is the outlier.

Root cause: verifier.md (line 8, 36, 37, 70, 109, 127) consistently says verifier only recommends closure; caller/orchestrator performs the actual close. beads-acceptance-review.md step 4 says 'close acceptance task only when gate is satisfied' — telling the verifier to close directly. These are mutually exclusive on the same action.

Correct behavior: verifier recommends, orchestrator closes. Supported by core-rules.md line 11 ('All beads writes belong to the orchestrator alone ... MUST NOT run bd close'), execution-orchestration.md lines 14 and 57, tasker.md line 37, reviewer.md line 13.

  3. create follow-up bugs/tasks for defects or missing coverage
  4. close acceptance task only when gate is satisfied

With:

Also add to the final line:

  With:    'Verifier should not modify tracker state directly and not silently reopen scope without a tracked issue.'

Do NOT change verifier.md — it is the correct implementation of the serialized-writes invariant.

title: architecture-review.md 'Output emphasis' duplicates mode-specific guidance already in SKILL.md
description:

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:

The fix for verifier.md is now tracked under claude-dev-tools-enb, which has been expanded to cover all 3 agent files (tasker.md, verifier.md, reviewer.md) with the same root cause and fix. Closing this bug as duplicate to avoid duplicate work.

title: architecture-review.md output section omits Verdict and contradicts SKILL.md required format
description:
File: plugins/complexity-review/skills/complexity-review/references/architecture-review.md
Section: ## Output emphasis
Problem: The 'Output emphasis' section prescribes a 4-item list (core model, main sources of accidental complexity, simplest credible alternative, complexity that is justified) that does not mention the Verdict field or Principle pressure points required by SKILL.md's 'Required review output' section. An agent following architecture-review.md exclusively would not know it must produce a Verdict (approve / approve with concerns / needs clarification / reject), Principle pressure points, or structured Findings. The two output specifications are inconsistent.
Fix: The 'Output emphasis' section should either (a) explicitly state it supplements the required output in SKILL.md and only lists the architecture-specific additions, or (b) include a reference back to SKILL.md's 'Required review output' section so reviewers know the Verdict and Findings structure still applies.
Found by: claude-dev-tools-sei.8

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
Verdict: REAL — rescoped to cover all 3 reference docs (same pattern in each).

Root cause: architecture-review.md, requirements-review.md, and code-pr-review.md each have a standalone 'Output emphasis' section that lists only mode-specific additions but does not reference SKILL.md's universal Required review output (Verdict, Principle pressure points, Findings, Open questions, What to remove/defer/simplify, What is justified). An agent that drops into a single reference doc without SKILL.md in context would produce a structurally incomplete review.

The reference docs are designed as supplements to SKILL.md, not self-contained specs. The correct fix is to add a one-line back-reference at the top of each Output emphasis section rather than duplicating the universal template.

Fix instructions — apply to all 3 reference files:

1. File: plugins/complexity-review/skills/complexity-review/references/architecture-review.md
   At the top of the 'Output emphasis' section, add:
   'These items supplement the Required review output in SKILL.md (Verdict, Principle pressure points, Findings, Open questions, What to remove/defer/simplify, What is justified); all standard sections still apply.'

2. File: plugins/complexity-review/skills/complexity-review/references/requirements-review.md
   Same addition at the top of its Output emphasis section.

3. File: plugins/complexity-review/skills/complexity-review/references/code-pr-review.md
   Same addition at the top of its Output emphasis section.

Reason: adding to each reference doc keeps SKILL.md as the single source of truth for the universal output template. Do NOT copy the universal template into the reference docs — that creates drift risk.

title: SKILL.md Prerequisites missing remote-sync gate from release-workflow.md Phase 1
description:
File: plugins/github-releases/skills/github-releases/SKILL.md
Section: Prerequisites
Problem: SKILL.md Prerequisites lists only two pre-checks (gh auth status, git status --porcelain, correct branch) but omits the third gate required by release-workflow.md Phase 1: verifying the local branch is in sync with the remote via 'git fetch origin && git diff HEAD origin/main --stat'. A user following only SKILL.md could start a release on a branch that is behind the remote, which release-workflow.md explicitly guards against.
Fix: Add a third bullet to the Prerequisites section: 'git fetch origin && git diff HEAD origin/main --stat returns no differences (local branch in sync with remote)'
Found by: claude-dev-tools-sei.10

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
Verdict: REAL.

Root cause: SKILL.md Prerequisites (lines 10-15) lists 3 checks but omits a 4th that both release-workflow.md Phase 1 (line 11) and quality-gates.md Gate 1 (lines 14-16) require: verifying local branch is in sync with remote via 'git fetch origin && git diff HEAD origin/main --stat'. 'You are on the correct branch' does not imply 'in sync with remote' — a local main can be behind, ahead, or diverged. The Prerequisites section is designed as a self-contained pre-flight list, so the omission is not rescued by the References section.

Fix instructions:
File: plugins/github-releases/skills/github-releases/SKILL.md
Section: Prerequisites (lines 10-15)

Replace the existing 3-bullet list:
  - `gh auth status` succeeds (GitHub CLI authenticated)
  - `git status --porcelain` returns empty (clean working tree)
  - You are on the correct branch

With:
  - `gh auth status` succeeds (GitHub CLI authenticated)
  - `git status --porcelain` returns empty (clean working tree)
  - You are on the correct branch and `git fetch origin && git diff HEAD origin/main --stat` returns no differences (local branch in sync with remote)

title: reviewer.md: 'bd comments add' is both prohibited and recommended
description:
File: plugins/beads-tasks/agents/reviewer.md
Section: Core Rules vs. Output Sizing
Problem: Line 13 (Core Rules) explicitly prohibits 'bd comments add': 'You MUST NOT run `bd create`, `bd update`, `bd close`, `bd comments add`, or `bd dep add`'. Line 97 (Output Sizing) explicitly recommends it: 'for minor suggestions, use `bd comments add` not new issues'. These two instructions directly contradict each other within the same file.
Fix: Remove 'bd comments add' from the prohibited list in Core Rules (since the agent is expected to propose comments — but they're returned to the caller, not applied directly), OR remove the recommendation on line 97 and replace it with 'for minor suggestions, return a tracker-ready comment draft to the caller rather than a new issue'. The prohibition in line 13 exists because the agent must not mutate tracker state directly — the 'Output Sizing' section should reinforce that pattern, not break it.
Found by: claude-dev-tools-sei.1

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
Verdict: REAL.

Root cause: Line 13 (Core Rules) explicitly prohibits running `bd comments add`. Line 97 (Output Sizing) says 'for minor suggestions, use `bd comments add` not new issues' — direct contradiction with the caller-owns-mutations rule.

Fix instructions:
File: plugins/beads-tasks/agents/reviewer.md
Section: ## When Reviewing Something That Is NOT a Beads Issue → ### Output Sizing (line 97)

Replace:
  - **Comments over beads** — for minor suggestions, use `bd comments add` not new issues

With:
  - **Comments over beads** — for minor suggestions, return a tracker-ready comment draft to the caller rather than a new issue draft

Reason: removes the forbidden command invocation, preserves the intent (prefer comments over new issues for minor findings), and aligns with the 'caller owns all tracker mutations' rule on line 13.

title: SKILL.md requirements routing stance omits 'remove' as first recommendation
description:
File: plugins/complexity-review/skills/complexity-review/SKILL.md
Section: Workflow routing table, Requirements review row, Default stance column
Problem: The default stance reads 'Reduce, defer, or narrow scope unless value is clearly proven' but references/requirements-review.md specifies the preferred recommendation order as: remove, defer, narrow, simplify, accept. 'Remove' is the first and strongest preference, but the routing table stance omits it and uses the vague term 'Reduce' which does not clearly map to removal.
Fix: Change 'Reduce, defer, or narrow scope unless value is clearly proven' to 'Remove, defer, or narrow scope unless value is clearly proven'
Found by: claude-dev-tools-sei.7

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
Verdict: REAL.

Root cause: SKILL.md routing table row 10 (requirements review, Default stance column) says 'Reduce, defer, or narrow' but requirements-review.md lists the preferred order as remove, defer, narrow, simplify, accept — 'remove' is the primary recommendation. 'Reduce' reads as 'shrink/trim' (i.e., narrow), not 'remove entirely'. SKILL.md itself uses 'removing' on line 19 and 'remove' on line 43, making the routing-table wording an isolated slip.

Fix instructions:
File: plugins/complexity-review/skills/complexity-review/SKILL.md
Location: routing table row for requirements review, Default stance column (line 10)

Replace:
  'Reduce, defer, or narrow scope unless value is clearly proven'

With:
  'Remove, defer, or narrow scope unless value is clearly proven'

One-word fix. No other changes needed.

title: Create 4 user-invocable skills from commands
description:
## Description
Create four new skills under `plugins/project-docs/skills/`, one per existing command. Each skill is the user-facing entry point; it delegates into the `project-docs` skill for all actual workflow logic.

## Instructions

Create the following four files. Each follows the beads-plan/beads-work frontmatter pattern exactly.

### `plugins/project-docs/skills/create-docs/SKILL.md`
```markdown
---
name: create-docs
description: "Create or initialize project docs baseline from scratch for the current repository."
user-invocable: true
disable-model-invocation: true
---

Load the `project-docs` skill.

Treat this as optional focus guidance:

$ARGUMENTS

Run the **create-docs** flow from the `project-docs` skill:

- Use this when the user clearly wants first-time docs setup or baseline creation.
- Create canonical docs surfaces and routing guidance for the current project.
- Report created files, intentionally skipped files (with reasons), and verification results.
```

### `plugins/project-docs/skills/init-or-update-docs/SKILL.md`
```markdown
---
name: init-or-update-docs
description: "Refresh or update existing project docs lifecycle guidance; falls back to creation only when docs are missing."
user-invocable: true
disable-model-invocation: true
---

Load the `project-docs` skill.

Treat this as optional focus guidance:

$ARGUMENTS

Run the **update-focused** docs lifecycle workflow from `project-docs`:

- Prefer this when docs already exist and need refresh or correction.
- Keep scope docs-only; do not edit source code files.
- If canonical docs are missing, fall back to baseline creation.
- If the user explicitly asks for greenfield docs setup, steer to `/create-docs`.
```

### `plugins/project-docs/skills/improve-doc/SKILL.md`
```markdown
---
name: improve-doc
description: "Improve docs structure and quality — split, merge, slim, or consolidate; may propose and apply edits (not read-only)."
user-invocable: true
disable-model-invocation: true
---

Load the `project-docs` skill.

Treat this as optional focus guidance:

$ARGUMENTS

Run the improve-doc workflow from the `project-docs` skill:

- If strong incident context is present, run the incident-driven targeted path.
- Otherwise, run discussion-first analysis (positives, negatives, proposals), ask what to improve before edits, and require explicit confirmation before aggressive removals, consolidation, or deletion.
- This skill is not read-only by default; it can propose and then apply docs changes based on user direction.
```

### `plugins/project-docs/skills/review-docs/SKILL.md`
```markdown
---
name: review-docs
description: "Review and validate project docs in read-only mode — returns findings with severity and suggested fixes, no file edits."
user-invocable: true
disable-model-invocation: true
---

Load the `project-docs` skill.

Treat this as optional focus guidance:

$ARGUMENTS

Run the **review/validate without editing** flow from the `project-docs` skill.

Hard contract:

- Keep this session read-only.
- Do not modify, create, rename, or delete files.
- Return findings with severity and evidence, and concrete improvement suggestions only.
```

## Files to Modify
- CREATE `plugins/project-docs/skills/create-docs/SKILL.md`
- CREATE `plugins/project-docs/skills/init-or-update-docs/SKILL.md`
- CREATE `plugins/project-docs/skills/improve-doc/SKILL.md`
- CREATE `plugins/project-docs/skills/review-docs/SKILL.md`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All four SKILL.md files exist under `plugins/project-docs/skills/<name>/`
- [ ] Each has `user-invocable: true` and `disable-model-invocation: true` in frontmatter
- [ ] Each delegates to the `project-docs` skill (not `coder-docs`)
- [ ] Each passes `$ARGUMENTS` as optional focus guidance

title: bug
description:
## Description
The rule "Do NOT use TodoWrite, TaskCreate, or markdown files for task tracking when beads is active" existed in beads-plan/SKILL.md and was removed during the refactor but never moved to a canonical location in beads-core. grep -rn "TodoWrite" plugins/beads-tasks/skills/ returns zero results.

## Where Discovered
claude-dev-tools-e8t acceptance review mandatory grep check.

## Expected vs Actual
Expected: TodoWrite prohibition appears in exactly one canonical file (core-rules.md is the right home — it owns all shared operational rules).
Actual: Zero occurrences across all skill files.

## Minimal Repro
grep -rn "TodoWrite" plugins/beads-tasks/skills/ → no output

## Impact
Any tasker or orchestrator session loading these skills has no instruction to avoid TodoWrite as a tracker substitute. The prohibition is silently dropped.

## Fix
Add the rule to plugins/beads-tasks/skills/beads-core/references/core-rules.md under the existing tracker rules section: "Do NOT use TodoWrite, TaskCreate, or markdown files for task tracking — beads is the tracker."
close_reason: Fixed: added 'Beads is the tracker' section to core-rules.md with TodoWrite prohibition.

title: README + install/troubleshooting docs for keep-awake-linux
description:
## Description

Write the user-facing README for the plugin. Mirror the structure of `plugins/github-releases/README.md` and `plugins/complexity-review/README.md`.

Parent epic: claude-dev-tools-yt7. Depends on claude-dev-tools-0vo (scaffold), claude-dev-tools-8ed (script), claude-dev-tools-guz (hooks), claude-dev-tools-8ry (slash command).

## File to create

`plugins/keep-awake-linux/README.md`

## Required sections

1. **What it does** — one-paragraph summary. Linux only. `systemd-inhibit`-based. Per-session aware.
2. **Why** — the gap: macOS plugins exist, native-Linux didn't (until now). cc-caffeine's Electron daemon vs this plugin's ~100-line bash + no daemon.
3. **Install** — `/plugin install keep-awake-linux@claude-dev-tools`. Note no manual `settings.json` edits needed.
4. **How it works** — hook → action table (copy from epic / task claude-dev-tools-guz). Explain `Notification` → release decision and the rationale.
5. **Manual control** — `/keep-awake on|off|status` slash command. CLI usage from a plain shell: `keep-awake start <id>`, `keep-awake force-on`, etc.
6. **Inspect state** — where markers live (`$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/claude-keep-awake/sessions/`), how to check the inhibitor (`systemd-inhibit --list`).
7. **Multiple sessions** — explain the model: stays awake while ANY session is busy; releases when ALL are idle.
8. **Requirements** — Linux + systemd-logind (`systemd-inhibit` present); `jq` optional (used by hook stdin parser, document if it's required vs optional based on what was implemented in claude-dev-tools-guz).
9. **Non-systemd hosts** — graceful no-op, never errors.
10. **Troubleshooting** —
    - Inhibitor not appearing? `systemd-inhibit --list | grep claude-keep-awake` while a session is busy. If nothing, check `keep-awake status` and `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/claude-keep-awake/`.
    - Hook errors? `~/.claude/logs/` and `claude --debug`.
    - Stale markers after crash? `keep-awake gc` (auto-runs on `SessionStart`).
11. **Uninstall** — `/plugin uninstall keep-awake-linux@claude-dev-tools`. Also: `keep-awake force-off` and remove `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/claude-keep-awake/`.
12. **Why not just `systemctl mask sleep.target`** — pasted-from-LLM approach the user originally got; explain why this plugin is preferable (no sudo, no reboot persistence, self-releasing, scoped to Claude work).
13. **Prior art** — link to cc-caffeine, wakey-wakey, hiroro caffeinate plugin, tngranados blog. Note none cover Linux natively.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] README exists at `plugins/keep-awake-linux/README.md`.
- [ ] All 13 sections present.
- [ ] Markdown renders cleanly (`mdformat --check` or visual review).
- [ ] All shell snippets have been actually executed by the implementer and produce the documented output.
- [ ] No broken links.

## Verification (how to test)

```bash
# Lint (if available)
markdownlint plugins/keep-awake-linux/README.md || true

# Run every shell snippet from the README; confirm each produces the stated output.

# Link check
grep -oE '(https?://[^ )]+)' plugins/keep-awake-linux/README.md | xargs -I{} curl -sSILo /dev/null -w '%{http_code} {}\n' {}
```

title: /keep-awake slash command (on / off / status)
description:
## Description

Expose a `/keep-awake` slash command for manual control: hold the machine awake for non-Claude work, release it, or inspect state. Thin wrapper over the `force-on` / `force-off` / `status` verbs.

Parent epic: claude-dev-tools-yt7. Depends on claude-dev-tools-8ed (helper script).

## File to create

`plugins/keep-awake-linux/commands/keep-awake.md`

(Use the existing `plugins/beads-tasks/` or `plugins/github-releases/` commands as the structural reference.)

## Subcommands

| Invocation | Action |
|---|---|
| `/keep-awake` or `/keep-awake status` | Run `keep-awake status` and report. |
| `/keep-awake on` | Run `keep-awake force-on`. |
| `/keep-awake off` | Run `keep-awake force-off`. |
| `/keep-awake gc` | Run `keep-awake gc` (manual reaper trigger). |

The command body should just `!keep-awake $ARGUMENTS` (or equivalent per the slash-command authoring docs) and print the result.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Command file exists and is discovered after `/plugin install`.
- [ ] `/keep-awake on` causes `systemd-inhibit --list` to show the inhibitor.
- [ ] `/keep-awake off` releases it (assuming no real Claude session is busy).
- [ ] `/keep-awake status` prints active sessions + inhibitor PID.

## Verification (how to test)

```bash
# After install
/keep-awake status      # released, 0 sessions
/keep-awake on
/keep-awake status      # 1 session named "manual"
systemd-inhibit --list | grep claude-keep-awake   # 1 entry
/keep-awake off
/keep-awake status      # released
```

Also: confirm `/keep-awake on` while a real Claude session is busy does NOT spawn a second inhibitor (helper is idempotent — task claude-dev-tools-8ed acceptance criteria).
